pmos

Product Metrics & Driver Trees — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Strategy. Choosing and structuring product metrics is two problems wearing one coat: picking the one number that stands for value, and then decomposing it into levers a team can actually pull. The output side is owned by the North Star Metric and the broader North Star Framework — a primary metric plus a constellation of inputs — with the term itself popularised by Sean Ellis and the framework built out at Amplitude by John Cutler and team. The decomposition side belongs to metric trees and driver trees, built backward from the North Star into two-to-four causal drivers per node — with Paul Levchuk's critique as the necessary counterweight. The vocabulary that organises all of it — leading versus lagging, input versus output, vanity versus actionable (Ries), guardrails and counter-metrics — is the working kit. The packaged frameworks (McClure's AARRR, Google's HEART, Croll & Yoskovitz's One Metric That Matters) are starting templates, not answers. And Goodhart's law is the gravity every metric system fights. Where the field genuinely splits — one North Star or a portfolio, how much the tree's math can be trusted — this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 16 verified sources.

What product metrics are actually for

A metric is a proxy. It stands in for something you care about but cannot observe directly — value delivered, a habit forming, a business compounding — and the entire craft of metric selection is about minimising the gap between the proxy and the real thing. Most metric debates that feel like disagreements about which number are really disagreements about what we are trying to stand in for, and surfacing that is half the work.

The literature converges on three jobs a metric system has to do at once, and they pull in different directions. It has to align — give a distributed org a shared definition of progress so a pod in one corner is not optimising against a pod in another. It has to steer — be sensitive enough to movement that a team can act on it inside a planning cycle rather than learning the verdict a year late. And it has to stay honest — resist the slow corruption that sets in the moment it becomes a target. A revenue number aligns beautifully and stays fairly honest but steers terribly; a feature-level click rate steers well but aligns nothing and corrupts fast. No single metric does all three, which is the structural reason the field invented frameworks, trees, and constellations rather than settling on one blessed KPI.

The second framing worth holding: metrics are not the strategy, they are the instrumentation of a strategy. Cutler's recurring complaint is that teams skip the strategy and treat the dashboard as if it were one — the "messy middle" where an org goes "from insanely high-level business goals and metrics straight to features on a roadmap" with no connecting model. Everything that follows in this primer is an attempt to build that connecting tissue: from the one number at the top, down through the drivers, to the work.

The North Star Metric

The North Star Metric is the field's answer to "if you could watch only one number, what should it be?" Amplitude's definition is the working one: the single measure that captures the "relationship between the customer problems your product team is trying to solve and the revenue you aim to generate." The load-bearing word is relationship — a good North Star sits at the hinge where customer value and business value are the same motion, so that moving it is good for the user and good for the company simultaneously. Where those two diverge, the metric is wrong.

The term was popularised by Sean Ellis — the same growth lineage that gave us "growth hacking" at Dropbox — and the recurring motivation Ellis points at is the disconnect between revenue growth and value actually delivered to customers. Classic exemplars carry the idea better than any definition: nights booked, not sign-ups; messages sent to friends, not registered accounts; weekly active rather than cumulative downloads. Each one chooses a unit of realised value over a unit of activity that merely correlates with intent.

Three properties separate a real North Star from a vanity headline. It should be a leading indicator of success rather than a lagging one — Amplitude is explicit that revenue itself makes a poor North Star because it confirms the past instead of predicting the future. It should be expressed as a rate or a count of value events, not a cumulative total that can only ever go up. And it should be one the team can plausibly influence through the product, not a market-wide number swamped by pricing, macro, or brand. The failure mode is choosing a metric that is easy to measure over one that is faithful to value — and a North Star chosen for convenience quietly redirects an entire org toward the wrong summit.

The North Star Framework: metric plus inputs

A lone number, however well chosen, is a scoreboard, not a steering wheel. The North Star Framework — developed at Amplitude with Cutler and team — fixes this by pairing the metric with "a set of three to five influential, complementary factors that you believe most directly affect your North Star Metric and that your team can directly influence through your product." Those are the inputs. The North Star is the output everyone is rowing toward; the inputs are the oars individual teams actually hold.

Cutler's framing is that a North Star is best understood not as a lonely figure but as "a constellation of variables that together serve as a leading indicator of sustainable, differentiated product growth." The inputs typically span complementary dimensions — breadth, depth, frequency, efficiency — so that no single team can game the whole by overdriving one axis. A common pattern: a product whose North Star is weekly engaged users decomposes into new activated users (breadth), returning users (frequency), and actions per session (depth), with each owned by a different pod.

Amplitude's own evolution is the cleanest case study and a useful confession. They ran Weekly Querying Users as their North Star, then recognised "this metric wasn't truly reflecting the type of impact we wanted" — querying was too narrow a slice of value — and migrated to Weekly Learning Users supported by three leading indicators (activated accounts, broadcasted learnings, consumption of learnings). The lesson is not that they picked wrong once; it is that the North Star is a living artefact that should be revisited as the product's definition of value sharpens. A team that has never changed its North Star in three years has either nailed it permanently or, far more likely, stopped looking.

Input versus output metrics

The input/output distinction is the single most useful lens in this whole topic, because it maps cleanly onto controllability. An output metric is the result you want — the North Star, retention, revenue. An input metric is something upstream that a team can move directly and that, if your causal model is right, drags the output along with it. The discipline, in Cutler's terms, is to refuse to stop at the output: walk it back to the inputs where the actual product work lives, or you are left admiring a number you cannot touch.

The practical test for an input metric is brutal and clarifying: could a single team change this in a quarter by doing product work? If yes, it is an input you can build a roadmap around. If no — if it only moves when the market moves or three other orgs cooperate — it is an output you should decompose further before assigning it to anyone. Goals set on outputs feel ambitious in a planning doc and produce learned helplessness in execution; goals set on the right inputs are where teams find leverage. The art is keeping the input genuinely connected to the output, which is exactly where metric trees come in — and exactly where they can mislead.

Leading versus lagging indicators

Closely related but not identical to input/output is the leading/lagging distinction, which is about time rather than controllability. Leading indicators "predict future performance and help drive your daily initiatives"; lagging indicators "reflect past performance to assess success and shape long-term strategy." In a SaaS frame, the lagging set is the familiar financial spine — MRR, ARPU, net revenue retention — standardised, trusted, and impossible to change retroactively. The leading set — activation rate, sessions per user, session duration — is softer and noisier but actionable today.

The trap practitioners fall into is treating "leading" as a synonym for "good" and "lagging" as a synonym for "stale." Both are necessary, and the relationship between them is the whole point. A leading indicator is only worth tracking if it correlates strongly with the lagging outcome you ultimately care about — Amplitude's example being that strong engagement (leading) should let you "expect to retain users and keep a high MRR" (lagging). When a leading metric climbs and its paired lagging metric never follows, you have not found a lever; you have found a coincidence, and continuing to optimise it is how teams ship a year of green dashboards and flat revenue. Validating the leading-to-lagging link — ideally causally, at minimum by watching the lag close historically — is the unglamorous work that separates a metric system from a wall of pretty charts.

Metric trees and driver trees

If the North Star Framework gives you a top metric and a flat set of inputs, the metric tree (used interchangeably with "driver tree" and "KPI tree") gives you the full recursive structure beneath it. Mixpanel defines it as "a hierarchical decomposition of business goals" that connects strategic priorities to daily team actions in one interconnected, multi-metric view. Levers Labs sharpens the definition: "an analytical model that takes metrics and their weighted relationships to represent the flow of inputs into a business and how they are transformed into outputs."

Two construction rules recur across the camp. First, you build it backward: start at the North Star at the apex and decompose downward, so every leaf traces — in Levers Labs' phrase — "a causal chain all the way back to each action's impact on the North Star." Second, you keep each split narrow. Mixpanel's guidance is to break a node into "two or three key primary drivers" rather than an exhaustive list — a tree where every node fans out to ten children is a spreadsheet wearing a tree costume and steers nothing.

The canonical example is the multiplicative one Mixpanel and the broader camp lean on: Revenue = Number of Customers × Average Revenue per Customer, with each factor decomposing again — customers into new plus retained, ARPC into price mix plus expansion. The value of the structure is threefold: it makes the team's theory of the business explicit and falsifiable; it lets you localise a problem ("revenue is flat because retention dropped, not because acquisition stalled"); and it gives two pods working different branches confidence they are not silently cancelling each other out. Levers Labs' deeper point is that the tree's real product is shared definitions — "metrics are only as useful as the clarity of their definitions and consensus about their importance" — the tree being the artefact that forces that consensus.

Decomposing a goal into levers

The reason to build a tree is to answer the question every planning cycle asks: given a target on the top metric, what do we actually do? Decomposition turns an output goal ("grow North Star 20%") into a portfolio of input bets ("lift activation 5 points, cut week-4 churn 2 points"), each ownable, each estimable, each falsifiable. This is where the metric structure stops being an analysis artefact and becomes a planning one.

Two decomposition styles coexist and are worth keeping distinct. Mathematical decomposition splits a metric into factors that multiply or sum back to it exactly — Revenue = Customers × ARPC. It is clean, auditable, and guaranteed to reconcile. Causal decomposition splits a metric into the things that drive it without necessarily summing to it — onboarding quality drives activation drives retention. It is messier and never reconciles to the penny, but it is where the real leverage usually hides. Levers Labs' three-step build — define the North Star, decompose into direct contributors, then identify correlative-or-causal influences — deliberately moves from the mathematical layer down into the causal one.

This is precisely where Levchuk's warning earns its place: the multiplicative split is seductive because it always balances, but balancing is not the same as explaining. He notes that high parent-child correlations are "mathematically inevitable" yet "operationally misleading," and that the clean math can completely mask negative correlations between sub-metrics — discounts lifting traffic while suppressing per-visitor revenue. A decomposition tells you what a metric is made of; it does not tell you which lever has the best return. That requires elasticity work, experiments, and judgement on top of the tree — the tree is the map, not the territory, and treating it as the territory is the most common senior-level mistake in this whole area.

Vanity versus actionable metrics

The oldest and still sharpest filter on any candidate metric is Eric Ries's vanity-versus-actionable cut, set out in his original essay and absorbed into the Lean Startup canon. Vanity metrics "might make you feel good, but they don't offer clear guidance for what to do" — total hits, cumulative downloads, billions of messages sent. The tell is that they only go up and they sever the link between cause and effect: knowing you had ten thousand hits tells you nothing about "what actions you took in the past that drove those visitors to you."

An actionable metric, by contrast, lets you tie a specific action to an observed result. Ries's three structural properties: it is per-customer (rates and cohorts, not aggregates — "metrics are people, too"), it is hypothesis-testing (with A/B split-tests as "the most actionable of all" because they confirm or refute a specific claim), and it is tied to your model (you track the customer behaviours that lead somewhere useful, not whatever is easy to count). ProductPlan's catalogue of the usual offenders is a useful checklist for the senior reviewer: raw pageviews, social followers, downloads, registered users, running customer totals — numbers that "look spectacular on the surface but don't translate to meaningful business results." The subtlety is that vanity is contextual, not intrinsic: registered users is vanity for a mature product judged on engagement, and a legitimate input metric for a pre-launch waitlist. The question is never "is this metric vanity?" but "is this metric vanity for the decision in front of us?"

Counter-metrics and guardrails

Every optimised metric distorts something adjacent, which is why mature metric systems pair their targets with metrics they are explicitly not trying to move — guardrails, also called counter-metrics. Mixpanel defines them as "secondary metrics you monitor while conducting experiments" that supply "an early warning of negative impact" — letting teams chase a primary win without quietly breaking something elsewhere.

The canonical pattern is local-win, global-loss. Push Stories engagement and cannibalise the main feed; lift conversion with an aggressive modal and tank week-two retention; speed a workflow and balloon page-load time. The guardrail is the metric that catches the externality: when optimising feature engagement, you watch overall engagement and load time as the things that must not regress. The design discipline is to pick guardrails that sit on the most likely failure axis for the specific bet — a guardrail that could never plausibly move is decoration, and the absence of a guardrail on a metric under heavy optimisation pressure is a near-guarantee that the team will eventually discover the damage from a different dashboard, months too late. Guardrails are the practical, per-experiment defence against the deeper structural problem the next section names.

Off-the-shelf frameworks: AARRR, HEART, OMTM

Before building a bespoke tree, most teams reach for a packaged framework, and three are worth knowing as templates rather than answers. AARRR — Dave McClure's "Pirate Metrics" — maps the user lifecycle as a funnel: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue. McClure's intent was anti-vanity from the start: to help startups "narrow their focus to only those metrics that can directly affect the health of their business." Amplitude's framing stresses it is a loop, not a one-way chute — "acquisition must drive activation, activation must feed retention, retention enables referrals" — so a team obsessing over the top of the funnel while the middle leaks is misreading the model.

HEART — from Google's Kerry Rodden and colleagues — is the quality-of-experience counterpart: Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task success. Its real contribution is the accompanying Goals-Signals-Metrics process — start from the goal, identify the behavioural signal that indicates progress, then pick the metric that quantifies the signal — which is the most reusable idea in the whole framework. Rodden's own caveat matters: "not all five elements will be relevant to every feature," so HEART is a menu, not a mandatory five-course meal.

One Metric That Matters — Croll and Yoskovitz's discipline from Lean Analytics — pushes the opposite way from a constellation: "at any given time, there's one metric you should care about above all else." The OMTM changes by business stage (empathy, stickiness, virality, revenue, scale) and behaves like a "squeeze toy" — optimising one constraint makes the next one bulge out and become the new focus. The tension between OMTM's radical focus and the North Star Framework's constellation is real and productive, and it surfaces again in the debates below.

Goodhart's law and the failure modes

Every metric system operates under one inescapable force, and it is worth stating in its sharpest form. Goodhart's law, in Marilyn Strathern's 1997 phrasing, is that "when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure" — a tightening of Charles Goodhart's 1975 monetary-policy observation that any statistical regularity collapses once pressure is placed on it for control. The moment you set a metric as a goal and attach consequences, people optimise the metric rather than the thing it stood for, and the proxy detaches from the reality.

The examples are bleakly instructive: hospitals targeting length-of-stay reductions discharge patients prematurely and drive readmissions; education systems under No Child Left Behind advance unprepared students to secure funding; the h-index decays as a quality signal once it becomes the thing scientists are judged on. In product, the same mechanism produces dark patterns optimised for an engagement number, support teams closing tickets fast rather than well, and growth hacks that spike sign-ups while poisoning retention. The standard mitigations all appear elsewhere in this primer: pair targets with guardrails so gaming one axis trips another; prefer a constellation over a single number so no one lever wins alone; and remember that the metric tree's clean math, per Levchuk, can hide the very surrogation Goodhart predicts. Goodhart is not a problem you solve once; it is a tax you pay continuously, and the best metric systems are designed to raise the cost of gaming rather than to pretend it away.

Choosing your metrics in practice

Synthesising the canon into a working sequence: start from the strategy and the stage, not the dashboard. Croll and Yoskovitz's stage-based OMTM is the right opening move precisely because it forces the question "what is most broken right now?" before any framework is imposed — a pre-product-market-fit team measuring revenue is as confused as a scaling team still measuring sign-ups.

From there, the assembly most of the canon converges on: pick a North Star that sits at the value/revenue hinge and reads as a leading, not lagging, indicator; surround it with three to five inputs your teams can actually move; build the tree downward so each input traces a causal chain to the top; instrument guardrails on the axes most likely to be sacrificed; and pressure-test every candidate against the vanity-versus-actionable cut and against Goodhart — ask not just "does this measure value?" but "what happens when a team games this?" The packaged frameworks earn their place as scaffolding: AARRR for lifecycle coverage, HEART's Goals-Signals-Metrics for the derivation discipline. The output of all this is not a longer dashboard; it is a shorter one with an explicit theory attached — a handful of numbers each team can defend, decompose, and act on.

Live debates and open questions

Several genuine tensions run through this topic, and treating any of them as settled is a mistake. One North Star or a portfolio? Croll and Yoskovitz's OMTM argues for radical single-metric focus; the North Star Framework argues for a primary metric plus a constellation of inputs. Practitioners genuinely disagree, and the honest reconciliation is contextual — a small team mid-crisis benefits from OMTM's focus; a scaled org with many pods needs the constellation to coordinate. There is no universal answer here.

How much can you trust the tree's math? The metric-tree camp (Mixpanel, Levers Labs) presents decomposition as a path to clarity; Levchuk argues the clean multiplicative math actively obscures causality, confounders, and mix shifts — that the tree can give a "dangerous illusion" of completeness. This is an active, unresolved argument about whether the tool's central feature is also its central risk. Use the tree, but distrust its precision.

Is the whole project doomed by Goodhart? A more radical critique holds that any metric, once targeted, corrupts — so heavy metric-driven management is self-defeating. The mainstream response (guardrails, constellations, raising the cost of gaming) treats Goodhart as a tax to manage rather than a wall to hit, but the tension is real and the skeptics are not obviously wrong. And the fast-moving frontier: as of 2026, AI-assisted analytics that auto-generate metric trees and surface "drivers" from correlation are arriving quickly; whether they sharpen causal thinking or industrialise the exact surrogation Levchuk and Goodhart warn about is still being figured out, and worth watching with skepticism.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Goal-setting & OKRs — metric trees feed objectives and key results; the input-vs-output distinction maps directly onto the question of whether a KR should be an output target or an input commitment.
  • Experimentation & A/B testing — guardrail metrics and Ries's "actionable" cut live here; the primary/guardrail pairing is an experiment-design decision before it is a metrics one.
  • Product strategy & vision — a North Star is the instrumentation of a strategy; choosing one is downstream of having a real strategy to instrument.
  • Product discovery & user research — the causal theories a metric tree encodes are exactly the assumptions discovery exists to test before you bet a roadmap on them.
  • Pricing & monetisation — the ARPC branch of any revenue tree opens directly onto packaging, price mix, and expansion strategy.
  • Growth & the AARRR funnel — lifecycle metrics are the operational layer beneath acquisition, activation, and retention work.

References

  1. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude frames the North Star Metric as a leading indicator of product value — "the relationship between the customer problems your product team is trying to solve and the revenue you aim to generate" — paired with three to five complementary inputs the team can directly influence.

  2. productcompass.pm ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Product Compass attributes the term 'North Star Metric' to Sean Ellis (who also coined 'growth hacking' at Dropbox) and Amplitude's research across 11,000+ companies into the three 'games' products play (attention, transaction, productivity).

  3. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude's own story of moving from Weekly Querying Users to Weekly Learning Users plus three leading indicators shows the constellation model in practice — a single primary metric is paired with inputs, and the metric itself evolves as the value definition sharpens.

  4. cutlefish.substack.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    John Cutler describes the North Star Framework as 'a constellation of variables that together serve as a leading indicator of sustainable, differentiated product growth' and names the 'messy middle' failure: jumping from exec dashboards straight to roadmap features without the connecting model.

  5. mixpanel.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Mixpanel defines a metric tree as 'a hierarchical decomposition of business goals' built backward from the North Star into 'two or three key primary drivers,' connecting strategic priorities to daily team actions in one interconnected view.

  6. leverslabs.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Levers Labs casts a metric tree as 'an analytical model that takes metrics and their weighted relationships to represent the flow of inputs into a business and how they are transformed into outputs,' built backward from the North Star to form 'a causal chain all the way back to each action's impact.'

  7. medium.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Paul Levchuk warns that metric trees create 'the dangerous illusion that the tree holds only levers that matter' — multiplicative decompositions make parent-child correlations 'mathematically inevitable' but 'operationally misleading,' masking confounders, cohort mix shifts, and negative correlations between sub-metrics.

  8. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude distinguishes leading indicators (predict future performance, drive daily initiatives — activation rate, sessions per user) from lagging indicators (confirm past results, shape long-term strategy — MRR, NRR), and stresses leading metrics are only useful when they correlate strongly with the lagging outcome.

  9. mixpanel.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Mixpanel defines guardrail (counter) metrics as 'secondary metrics you monitor while conducting experiments' that act as 'an early warning of negative impact' — e.g. watching main-feed engagement or page-load time while optimising a new feature so a local win doesn't cause a global loss.

  10. tim.blog ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Eric Ries's original guest essay separates vanity metrics ('total hits', cumulative counts that 'make you feel good but don't offer clear guidance') from actionable metrics — per-customer, hypothesis-testing numbers, with A/B split-tests 'the most actionable of all' because they confirm or refute a specific hypothesis.

  11. productplan.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    ProductPlan catalogs the usual vanity metrics — raw pageviews, social followers, downloads, registered users, running customer totals — as numbers that 'look spectacular on the surface but don't translate to meaningful business results' and 'aren't related to anything you can control or repeat.'

  12. productplan.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    ProductPlan attributes AARRR 'Pirate Metrics' to Dave McClure (500 Startups) and lays out the five lifecycle stages — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — created to help startups 'narrow their focus to only those metrics that can directly affect the health of their business.'

  13. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude treats AARRR as a full-funnel loop where 'acquisition must drive activation, activation must feed retention, retention enables referrals,' steering teams away from vanity metrics toward the numbers that move business health at each stage.

  14. productplan.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    ProductPlan documents Google's HEART framework (Kerry Rodden et al.) — Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task success — paired with the Goals-Signals-Metrics process, noting 'not all five elements will be relevant to every feature,' so teams pick the relevant subset.

  15. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Goodhart's law — Marilyn Strathern's 1997 phrasing 'when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure' (from Charles Goodhart's 1975 monetary-policy observation) — is illustrated by gamed targets: hospital length-of-stay driving premature discharge, NCLB advancing unprepared students, the h-index decaying once weaponised.

  16. leananalyticsbook.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Croll & Yoskovitz's One Metric That Matters argues 'at any given time, there's one metric you should care about above all else,' changing by stage (empathy, stickiness, virality, revenue, scale); a 'squeeze toy' — optimising it makes the next constraint bulge out and become the new OMTM.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_product-metrics-and-driver-trees.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
